claim: FreeRTOS Kernel v11.1.0 is the active LTS release, supported until 2026-06-30.
multi_causal_factors: AWS stewardship of FreeRTOS LTS program
Stability requirements for safety-critical embedded deployments

claim: ESP-IDF v6.0 was released on 2026-03-19, introducing breaking changes from v5.x.
quote: We're excited to announce the long-awaited release of ESP-IDF 6.0!
multi_causal_factors: Espressif annual cadence release cycle
ESP32-P4 and ESP32-C6 new silicon support requirements

claim: Zephyr RTOS v4.4.0-rc1 is the latest release candidate as of March 2026; prior stable was v4.2.0 (August 2025).
quote: The first release candidate for Zephyr 4.4.0 has been tagged
multi_causal_factors: Linux Foundation semi-annual release cadence
Growing adoption in Nordic nRF and STM32 ecosystems

claim: MISRA C:2025 prohibits dynamic memory allocation in safety-critical code (Rule 21.3) and now addresses AI-generated code and C24 standard.
quote: MISRA C:2025 demonstrates how safety standards must evolve alongside technology, addressing AI-generated code and the growing interest in Rust.
multi_causal_factors: Rise of LLM code generation in embedded toolchains
C23/C24 standard ratification in October 2024

claim: FreeRTOS configCHECK_FOR_STACK_OVERFLOW Method 2 paints task stacks with a known value (0xA5) and validates the last 16 bytes on context switch.
quote: When a task is first created its stack is filled with a known value. The RTOS kernel can check the last 16 bytes within the valid stack range.
multi_causal_factors: Software-only detection without MPU hardware
Low overhead on Cortex-M3/M4 without TrustZone

claim: Zephyr 2026 research disaggregates RTOS subsystems into mutually untrusted components with zero run-time software TCB, offering a security-by-design foundation.
quote: We present a soft real-time operating system based on Zephyr that has no run-time software TCB. All subsystems including scheduler, allocator and DMA drivers are fully untrusted.
multi_causal_factors: Growing IoT attack surface
Supply-chain firmware integrity requirements

claim: Embedded Rust on Cortex-M uses no_std + cortex-m-rt crate for bare-metal startup, with core::sync::atomic replacing volatile for ISR-shared state.
quote: cortex-m-rt provides the necessary runtime including the entry point and interrupt handling; panic-halt halts the processor on unrecoverable error.
multi_causal_factors: Rust ownership model eliminates data races at compile time
Ferrocene qualified Rust toolchain for safety-critical deployment

claim: EmbedCGen benchmark (2026) tests LLM bare-metal C code generation across 500 problems with ARM GCC cross-compilation and Renode simulation verification.
quote: EmbedCGen comprises 500 problems across five difficulty levels, from basic peripheral control to DMA and state machine coordination. Generated code must satisfy HAL conventions and hard real-time constraints.
multi_causal_factors: LLM code generation now evaluated against hardware-accurate simulators
Gap between syntactic correctness and timing-correct embedded code

claim: FreeRTOS on ESP32 imposes stricter real-time constraints than generic desktop OS environments, requiring ISR-safe API variants.
quote: FreeRTOS on ESP32 may impose stricter real-time constraints and OS environments.
multi_causal_factors: Xtensa LX7 dual-core with asymmetric interrupt affinity
WiFi/BT softMAC coexistence with application ISRs

claim: A FreeRTOS mode-transition barrier on ESP32 at 240 MHz costs ~1,942 cycles (8.09µs), establishing a measurable WCET baseline for task synchronization overhead.
quote: The mode-switching protocol introduced a measured overhead of 1,942.5 cycles (median) for the two-phase FreeRTOS barrier. At 240 MHz, 1,942 cycles correspond to 8.09µs.
multi_causal_factors: Dual-core FreeRTOS barrier requires semaphore + pointer reassignment sequence
Interrupt escape from suppression window adds outlier latency
